quotes.quote: I have no interest in swing speed training but want to take a more holistic approach to health and golf (core, flexibility, strength) and hope it leads to distance gains.
I am after more distance and accuracy off tee.
I hit one 287 right down middle today and several over 260. I am after more distance and accuracy off tee.
I'd love to be more consistent, Danny, but really, I'd also love to gain a lot more yardage.
I wish I could get some decent coaching! Can hit most Wedges well
I wish I could carry a 4-iron 175yds.
Since then and with practice my long irons have improved no end with probably less shaft lean and divot but a more consistent strike tho I do wish I had a bit more distance

I have been playing for about five years. Just a weekend warrior and sometimes during the week. I have been fitted for clubs and love my clubs. I hit the ball very straight. Problem is I can't hit it far at all.
I wish I could reply to everyone. It's refreshing to know about my technique. I think getting in better shape and working on my technique and mechanics will help.
OP, you have mad speed to unlock, you don't have to get in better shape. You can gain mad distance with just a better swing.
Anyway I will work on getting better. Bought a net for my backyard and going to get some new lessons and not stop until I get my technique down.
I am telling you, that you already have speed in you that just needs to be unlocked. It's not about being skinnier, but just being more athletic and making a better move at the ball.
Improve your mechanics. Any able bodied male under the age of 50 should be able to drive the ball at least 250.
I'd suggest u give Superspeed a try. I've been working on the system for the past 3 months and seen significant gains in distance across the board.
The challenge now is to remain as calm as I can regardless of who I am playing with and how we are all playing.
I am Getting close to putting this together and find myself really focusing on course a little better.
I did speed training, working out and flexibility training for about 8 months. I went from 97 to 103 club speed.
Last night I played 9 with my kid. I used driver 3x and hit fairway about 260-270 on 1st one, 2nd time was couple yards off the front on a hole that had a 286 marker. I'll take those at 47yrs. old.
I know my body and I see no physical limitation that should restrict me from getting at least to Champions Tour averages (277) rather than say I'm limited to LPGA average (low 250s).
Many others and I would kill to average 268 yards off of the tee.
I really try not to feel a need to chase distance, it comes natural when I swing easy and calm.
I can really jack up swing speed and hit a few serious bombs out to 310 or even 330.
I am quite capable of shooting 82 today then 96 tomorrow. In fact, two days ago I shot 88, 46 on front and 42 on back, that's with 4 doubles on front and 1 on back.
I am only hitting like 43% of fairways, my avg has increased to 278 off tee, but I need to be more relaxed and swing a bit easier and get my fairway hits up to 60% or close to that anyway.
I am gonna hit more greens from 80 out.
Travis Graham I wish I could do that in vr consistently.
Golf is your sport, stick with it.
I think any male in their thirties without a disability can hit driver a lot more than 190.
I would be shocked if the OP didn't gain at least 6-10 mph without trying to hit it any harder within one in person lesson from a teacher.
This is a load of crap IMHO. Asking to swing 115+..sure not possible for everyone. But there is no reason someone in their 30s shouldn't be able to swing ~100mph unless they have a legit disability.
Since most club head speed is from proper loading and unloading of the wrists and neither of those things are happening, you have speed to gain.
We all want more speed and distance!
Most golfers try to hit the ball further by swinging harder or longer - but what if the real secret to distance is actually swinging shorter?

I have been playing golf for 50 years. Since I obsoleted my old Titleist 975D driver I have been trying new drivers every other year. This year I bought a Ping 400cc LST with their stock 65g shaft. I have gained 15 yards minimum with this driver.

Distance isn't Just About Ball Speed - it's also about launch and spin. If a given design allows the golfer to hit the ball higher with less spin, distance will increase even if ball speed is constant.
I wish I could promise monster distance gains with each product update, but that isn't happening.

I have always hit it by 50% of the guys I encounter, even with another 25% and then there's the 25% that blow it by me. I'd be lying if I said I didn't subconsciously reach for extra at times when I'm playing with guys that have a lot of firepower.
I'm in my 40s and happy if I poke it out there to 220-240.
I am 70+ and all about the score walking off 18. Playing to a 4, low of 2, still carrying it 240-245+yds, and can out play most younger guys that are fascinated with hitting driver.
Get yourself down to the teens, then practice your short game until you love putting and then, as a 10 handicapper, it's maybe the time to start discussing forward shaft lean.
In my humble opinion, most people should get down to 18 or less if they practice the right things.
Fix your swing, and you'll get more power naturally.
It takes effort, the same as anything else in life. Improve your technique, improve your fitness. Commit to it. Swing changes take months and years.
My revelation on sequencing happened several years back while watching an LPGA tournament. Can't remember the player, but something just clicked and I grabbed my driver and went to the garage.
I literally felt like I was flinging the clubhead through with no effort. It created a lot of speed, but didn't feel like much effort.
It has taken years but I've gradually come to understand that all of the things my body does when I ask it for more distance are counter-productive.
Making a full turn with good tempo and sequencing do add speed but they don't feel fast.
I struggle with the same issue, though I am 65. I too tried everything. I have come to accept it is what it is, and if I play the appropriate tees I can still have fun.
I have also worked very hard on my short game...you don't need to be young or athletic to develop a good short game, and a good short game is a real stroke saver.
I'm 36 years old, 6'1 285. And I average about 275 yards, with the occasional 300 yarder. And since really working on my swing I've dropped my handicap down from 18+ to 5 in 20 months now.
